title: How to do distributed locking
author: Martin Kleppmann
note: Shows why Redlock is unsafe under process pauses and clock skew, and introduces fencing tokens — monotonically increasing counters the storage service checks to reject stale lock holders.

title: The network is reliable
author: Kyle Kingsbury and Peter Bailis
note: Informal survey of real-world network partitions at Microsoft, Google, Amazon and others (also in ACM Queue), including GC pauses and NIC firmware bugs that mimic partitions.

title: Spanner: Google's Globally-Distributed Database
author: James C. Corbett et al. (Google)
note: The OSDI 2012 paper introducing TrueTime, which exposes clock uncertainty as an explicit interval (using GPS and atomic clocks) to provide externally consistent distributed transactions.

title: Navigating the 8 fallacies of distributed computing
author: Alex Diaconu (Ably)
note: Walks through all eight of Peter Deutsch's fallacies — the network is reliable, latency is zero, bandwidth is infinite, etc. — with concrete engineering mitigations for each.

title: Jepsen Analyses
author: Kyle Kingsbury (Jepsen, LLC)
note: In-depth safety analyses of dozens of databases and coordination services under network partitions, process crashes, and clock skew, documenting stale reads, split brain, and data loss.

title: Testing Distributed Systems w/ Deterministic Simulation
author: Will Wilson (Strange Loop 2014)
note: Explains how FoundationDB runs its entire distributed database in a deterministic simulator, injecting network, disk, and machine failures so any bug-triggering schedule can be replayed exactly.

title: Learn TLA+
author: Hillel Wayne
note: Free, beginner-friendly guide to Lamport's TLA+ formal specification language, with a linear core track plus examples of model-checking concurrency and distributed protocol designs.

title: MIT 6.5840: Distributed Systems
author: MIT PDOS (Robert Morris, Frans Kaashoek et al.)
note: Graduate course (formerly 6.824) with open lectures, readings, and Go programming labs building MapReduce, Raft, and a sharded fault-tolerant key-value store.
